title: Wax-resist paste paper
subtitle: Applying melted batik wax to cartridge paper before painting to create crackle-line resist patterns and textured colour fields
excerpt: Wax resist on paper works on the same principle as batik on fabric: wax applied before painting blocks pigment from reaching the paper surface beneath it. On cartridge paper, the technique produces two distinct visual effects, a clean hard edge where the wax was brushed on flat, and a crackle-line network where the waxed sheet was deliberately crumpled before painting. The wax is removed afterwards by ironing between blotting sheets.
sourceNotes: Craft tradition; techniques for wax-resist paper craft are documented widely in mid-20th-century studio craft literature. No single canonical source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique in which melted wax is applied to paper or fabric before painting. The wax is hydrophobic, it repels water-based paint, so any area covered in wax remains the colour of the base surface after the paint layer is applied.

The network of fine lines that appears when a waxed sheet is crumpled before painting. Crumpling fractures the solidified wax layer; paint seeps through the cracks and deposits colour along the fracture lines. The paraffin component of batik wax crackles more readily than pure beeswax.

The final step of removing wax from the painted sheet by sandwiching it between layers of blotting paper and pressing with a warm iron. The heat melts the wax and the blotting paper absorbs it. Repeat with clean blotting paper until no wax transfers.

content.text: Melted wax is a fire risk if overheated. Use a double boiler, never a direct flame, and keep the temperature below 80°C. Work on a heat-proof mat and keep a lid nearby to smother any flame. Do not leave molten wax unattended.

on paper works best on a smooth, non-absorbent surface: 130 gsm cartridge or hot-press watercolour paper. Highly absorbent paper pulls the wax into the fibres rather than leaving it on the surface, and the resist effect is weak. Apply the wax while it is fully liquid and clear, once it turns white or milky in the pot, it has cooled too far and will sit on the surface in a thick, uneven layer.

items.name: Self-healing cutting mat, A3 (heat-resistant working surface)

content.text: Melt the wax pellets in the double boiler until clear and fully liquid. Test the temperature by brushing a small amount onto scrap paper: it should spread easily and turn transparent immediately as it cools. If it stays white, the wax is too cool.

Work quickly, the wax cools within seconds of leaving the pot. Lay the paper flat on the cutting mat and brush the wax in smooth, broad strokes. The wax must penetrate through the paper to resist well; on a good sheet, hold it up to a window after waxing and the waxed areas should be translucent.
: once the wax is fully cold and hard (5-10 minutes), crumple the sheet firmly in both hands, then smooth it flat again. Crumple in several directions to produce an even network of cracks.

Apply watercolour washes over the waxed paper in one smooth pass, do not scrub back and forth, as this can push pigment under the wax edge. The paint beads and sheets off the waxed areas, settling only on the unwaxed paper and into the crackle lines.

Once the paint is dry, remove the wax: sandwich the sheet between two sheets of blotting paper and press with a warm iron (synthetic setting, no steam). The wax melts and transfers into the blotting paper. Repeat with fresh blotting paper until no further wax transfers.
takes 3-5 passes; some residue always remains in the paper fibres, which gives the surface a slight translucency and a soft, slightly waxy hand.

items.symptom: The wax sits on the surface and doesn't penetrate, the resist is patchy
items.cause: The wax was applied too cool; it solidified before penetrating the fibres.
items.fix: Reheat the wax until clear and liquid. Work with the paper on a slightly warm surface, a sheet of warm cartridge underneath helps slow the cooling rate.

items.symptom: No crackle lines appear even after heavy crumpling
items.cause: The wax blend is too flexible, too much beeswax relative to paraffin.
items.fix: Use a standard batik wax blend (70% paraffin, 30% beeswax). Pure beeswax does not crack; it bends. Pure paraffin cracks too readily and the paint seeps through large areas rather than fine lines.
